reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

      "ocr_excerpt": "“Danxi said…” The textual life of medical ideas on madness, 1360-1530 Danxi said that dian belongs to Yin and kuang to Yang, that dian is excessive joy and kuang excessive anger. He also stated that the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang. 丹溪謂癲屬陰 狂屬陽 癲多喜 狂多怒 又云重陰者癲 重陽者狂 Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 in Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556) $ ^{1} $ Chapters 3 and 4 traced a momentous transformation in the etiology of madness from Wind to internal Fire and mucus between the twelfth and the fourteenth century. The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) was not a period of strong epistemological breaks in medicine, but one in which physicians re-orchestrated earlier approaches to form new doctrinal and therapeutic emphases. Xu Chunfu above was citing the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法, a composite work compiled and printed in his own Huizhou Prefecture (Anhui) in the early 1480s and that quickly became the most influential work attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (\"Danxi\" 丹溪: 1282-1358). This and the next chapter show how the development of Ming medical thinking on dian and kuang was shaped by three important trends that are well symbolized by Xu's quotation: the rising influence of precepts attributed to Danxi, th ..."

      "page_no": 156,
      "ocr_excerpt": "passages were bundled together in increasingly sophisticated sections on madness in the Danxi corpus, as well as in the works of the physicians who admired Danxi's works. For my argument to be convincing, I must therefore demonstrate the ascendancy of Danxi precepts. Discursive dominance requires a certain density of social diffusion. Through texts and people (notably Zhu's own disciples), Zhu Zhenheng's medicine first spread like a rhizome along arteries such as the Grand Canal, feeding on social connections and networks of teachers and readers, growing more densely in some places than others, and presenting different faces in the many areas where it flourished. The Danxi corpus became dominant insofar as many important debates between medical specialists concerned the way in which Danxi's legacy should be interpreted. Physicians appropriated the name \"Danxi\" as a symbol of authority or used it as a foil to make their own doctrinal emphases appear more distinctive. The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on ..."

      "ocr_excerpt": "that made texts meaningful in particular teaching traditions, and about the process by which practitioners developed their skills through a mix of readings, instruction by one or several masters or seniors, and interaction with real patients. All these caveats make our understanding of received texts all the more important. Intertextuality, zhezhong hermeneutics, and deliberate syncretism To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—i ..."

      "ocr_excerpt": "This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), ..."

      "ocr_excerpt": "### 5.2 Madness in Lou Ying's 樓英 (1320-1400) $ ^{12} $ Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396) The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the \"Immortals' crag\" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $ Lou Ying started to collect materials for his summa of current medical doctrine and healing methods in the 1360s and completed a first version in 1380; he achieved the final version in 1396. Lou did not ha ..."

    {
      "page_no": 162,
      "ocr_excerpt": "Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $ Textual montages like Lou's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning were not amateurish endeavors. Composite texts professing to cover all aspects of illness and medicine were actually a new genre of medical writing at which Zhu Zhenheng's disciples excelled. $ ^{18} $ Many books claiming to be for beginners were sophisticated digests of complex medical teachings; they were not easy “primers,” but points of entry into the intricate traditions they summarized. cluster.chapter_title: “Danxi said…” The textual life of medical ideas, 1360-1530

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them proposed different interpretations of a common body of texts that included the Danxi corpus, but also the Inner Canon and the works of Liu Wansu and Li Gao. $ ^{11} $ Throughout, I try to recreate the doctrinal debates that make changes in their approaches to madness intelligible.

The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: appended one or two recipes under each illness. $ ^{80} $ Zhao had first seen the Recorded Sayings in the Suzhou area some years after Zhu's death in 1358, and again ten years later at many friends' places (遍見於朋友處), under a new title: Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao 丹溪藥要). Lamenting that this book was of such low quality, yet was circulating widely by virtue of its attribution (欲駕其名以傳諸徒 豈先生之意哉), Zhao decided to re-edit it in a question-and-answer format and to enhance it with his own comments. The result was Questions on the Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao buowen 或問), which, to judge from the date of its preface, was completed in 1384. $ ^{81} $

To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $

History of the Ming (138: 4137) indicates that in the early Zhengtong reign (in or soon after 1436), Yang “dispatched military and civil officials to zhenfu Jiangxi, Huguang, Henan, and Shandong” (分遣文武鎮撫江西湖廣 河南 山東). In 1438 Yang Shiqi had been named Junior Preceptor (少師), a prestige title ranked 1b. Duxian 都憲 is not listed in Hucker 1985, but it probably referred to a post in the Censorate. A sample mistake in Chen's edition: “Wang Andao” 王安道 (Zhu's disciple Wang Liu) was turned into “Yu'an daoren” 玉安道人 (“Taoist master Yu'an”; juan 1, “Wind strokes,” “neiyin si zhongfeng lun” 内因似中風論; LiuChYQ: 117A). 73 Angela Leung (2003b: 132) postulated that Yang's writing a preface for it showed that the Yijing xianxue 醫經小學 was “an important introductory textbook during the early Ming,” but it appears that Yang's preface enabled rather than recognized the influence of this text.

Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

He also stated that the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang. 丹溪謂癲屬陰 狂屬陽 癲多喜 狂多怒 又云重陰者癲 重陽者狂 Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 in Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556) $ ^{1} $ Chapters 3 and 4 traced a momentous transformation in the etiology of madness from Wind to internal Fire and mucus between the twelfth and the fourteenth century. The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) was not a period of strong epistemological breaks in medicine, but one in which physicians re-orchestrated earlier approaches to form new doctrinal and therapeutic emphases. Xu Chunfu above was citing the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法, a composite work compiled and printed in his own Huizhou Prefecture (Anhui) in the early 1480s and that quickly became the most influential work attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 ("Danxi" 丹溪: 1282-1358). This and the next chapter show how the development of Ming medical thinking on dian and kuang was shaped by three important trends that are well symbolized by Xu's quotation: the rising influence of precepts attributed to Danxi, the importance of Huizhou as a center of medical theorizing and publication, and the profound intertextuality of the elite medical tradition.

passages were bundled together in increasingly sophisticated sections on madness in the Danxi corpus, as well as in the works of the physicians who admired Danxi's works. For my argument to be convincing, I must therefore demonstrate the ascendancy of Danxi precepts. Discursive dominance requires a certain density of social diffusion. Through texts and people (notably Zhu's own disciples), Zhu Zhenheng's medicine first spread like a rhizome along arteries such as the Grand Canal, feeding on social connections and networks of teachers and readers, growing more densely in some places than others, and presenting different faces in the many areas where it flourished. The Danxi corpus became dominant insofar as many important debates between medical specialists concerned the way in which Danxi's legacy should be interpreted. Physicians appropriated the name "Danxi" as a symbol of authority or used it as a foil to make their own doctrinal emphases appear more distinctive. The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. Intertextuality, zhezhong hermeneutics, and deliberate syncretism To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $ Trying to prove the intertextuality of the Chinese medical literature may seem like laboring the obvious. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $ The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them p ...

### 5.2 Madness in Lou Ying's 樓英 (1320-1400) $ ^{12} $ Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396) The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $ Lou Ying started to collect materials for his summa of current medical doctrine and healing methods in the 1360s and completed a first version in 1380; he achieved the final version in 1396. Lou did not have all the books he consulted at hand at any given time. He probably kept adding to his text as he consulted more and more works, something that was made more convenient by the proximity of Hangzhou ( ...

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $ Textual montages like Lou's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning were not amateurish endeavors. Composite texts professing to cover all aspects of illness and medicine were actually a new genre of medical writing at which Zhu Zhenheng's disciples excelled. $ ^{18} $ Many books claiming to be for beginners were sophisticated digests of complex medical teachings; they were not easy “primers,” but points of entry into the intricate traditions they summarized. Students of medicine found these compilations all the more useful since readers could not easily access all the texts these works cited. Lou Ying's compilation was concerned with medical practice, not abstract doctrine. As a reasoned anthology of citations, the point of the Systematic Outline of Medical Learning was not to reconcile all medical approaches into a perfectly ...

Zhongjing [Zhang Ji] is detailed on external contamination that affects the interior and the surface or Yin and Yang; Danxi distinguishes himself on inner harm of Blood or Qi [that causes] depletion or repletion; Dongyuan [Li Gao] supports and protects central Qi; Hejian [Liu Wansu] removes the old to create the new; Mr. Qian [Yi] distinguishes clearly between the five Yin viscera; Dairen [Zhang Congzheng] is well versed in the three methods [vomiting, purging, and sweating]. Formularies from past ages are abundant: we can simply say that each has its strengths. 仲景詳外感於表裡陰陽 丹溪獨內傷於血氣虛實 東垣扶護中氣 河間推陳 致新 錢氏分明五臟 戴人熟施三法 凡歷代方書甚衆 皆各有所長耳 Since the same symptoms could occur for different reasons, it was plausible that many texts could contain useful methods of treatment. $ ^{19} $ “The benighted fail to appreciate this principle: their reckless trying [of recipes] on approximate illnesses has led many to an unjust and premature death” (昧者不悟是理 妄試疑似之病 每致夭橫者不少矣). Armed with a definition of illness as a multifaceted and ever-changing entity that was typical of Zhu Zhenheng's followers, $ ^{20} $ Lou proposed an elegant solution to the controversial issue of whether “old recipes” could cure “current illnesses.” “Given that an illness with a single name [can require] different methods of treatment, when people use recipes from past ages to treat illness, their success or failure depends on wheth ...
